topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud, instantly stealing the air from my lungs. My fingers, hovering over the keyboard, froze mid-scroll. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah is cancelled," the subject line read. A slow, creeping cold began to spread through my chest, tightening my throat until it felt impossible to draw a full breath. I reread the message three times, searching for a typo, a mistake, anything to suggest this was a prank. But the words were stark, final. A strange, hollow ache settled in my stomach, and I had to stand up, my legs feeling strangely unsteady. I walked to the window, staring blankly at the street below, the cheerful decorations we’d spent weeks planning suddenly seeming garish and cruel.

[story 2]

I stared at my phone screen, the notification blinking mockingly. The silence in my apartment was deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears. *Cancelled.* The single word echoed in the empty space where excitement had just been. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I started pacing the small room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled, as if trying to physically shake the news away. Every casual sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—felt amplified, pressing down on me. I wanted to disappear, to sink into the floorboards, anywhere but here, feeling exposed under the harsh glare of the overhead light.

[story 3]

When I finally read the confirmation, the color seemed to drain from my face. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a physical tightening, a constricting sensation around my ribs. I let out a small, involuntary sound—a choked whisper that was barely audible. I dropped the phone onto the rug, the plastic clattering loudly in the quiet room. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, and I found myself running them through my hair repeatedly, a desperate, futile gesture. I stood perfectly still for a long moment, my gaze fixed on a spot on the wall, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of deflation washing over me. The world felt tilted, and I couldn't seem to find my footing.
